banner: Week 2 · Prove It — Points, Lines, Rays & Segments
essential_question: Can you defend the difference — not just name it?
objective: Justify why a line has infinitely many points and how rays carry direction, review the week's Math-U-See practice, and pass the mastery checkpoint.

content.prompt: Without looking, draw and correctly symbolize a point, a line, a ray, and a segment. Then check against your concept note.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 1A & 1B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 1A/1B. We check the tricky ones: opposite rays, is AB included in BC, and the = vs ≅ boxes. Fix any you missed and note WHY.

content.heading: Defend two claims
bullets: Claim 1: a line has infinitely many points. Argue it: between any two points you can always find another.
Claim 2: ray AB and ray BA are different. Argue it from the endpoint.
Claim 3: segment AB and segment BA are the same. Argue it from 'the piece between'.

content.preview: Next week, Lesson 2: planes & sets — where these figures live.

success_criteria.text: I can symbolize and identify all four figures without help
I can justify why a line has infinitely many points
I can explain how endpoints give rays direction but not segments

questions.prompt: A geometric figure with no dimensions (no length, no width) is a…
questions.explain: A point marks a location only — no length or width.

questions.prompt: The symbol with an arrow on only one end, written endpoint-first, is a…
questions.explain: One arrow, one endpoint = a ray; the endpoint is written first.

questions.prompt: Which pair names the SAME figure?
questions.explain: A segment is the piece between two ends; naming order doesn't matter. Ray AB and ray BA point opposite ways.

questions.prompt: How many points lie on a line 3 cm long?
questions.explain: Length has nothing to do with it — any line (or segment) contains infinitely many points.

questions.prompt: Draw line EF, then mark a point G on it that is collinear with E and F.
questions.answer: A line through E and F with G placed anywhere on that same line.
questions.explain: Rubric: a straight line labeled E and F (1 pt) + a third point G on that line, i.e. collinear (1 pt).

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain to a younger student why a line has infinitely many points.
questions.answer: Between any two points on the line you can always squeeze another point, and you can keep doing that forever — so there is no end to how many points fit.
questions.explain: Full credit: names 'between any two there's always another' or an equivalent never-ending argument.

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 1 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one idea you'd want to revisit.
